metadata.title: UDL Choice Board Audit: Same Demand, Different Path
metadata.description_blurb: Choice boards fail when one path requires less thinking — Universal Design for Learning means same intellectual demand, different access route, and Claude cannot verify demand equivalence without the teacher.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: She asked Claude to draft a UDL choice board for her argument writing unit — three ways students could demonstrate understanding. Three options arrived: a written essay, a recorded presentation, and an annotated concept map. She published it. By week three, almost every student was choosing the concept map. The map was shorter and less demanding than the other two. The choice board had reproduced the problem it was supposed to solve. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, UDL choice board for argument writing unit

props.command: Draft a UDL choice board for my unit on argument writing — three ways students can demonstrate their understanding

beats.narration_text: Universal Design for Learning means multiple means of representation, action, and engagement — not easier versus harder. The principle is the same intellectual demand, different access route. A student who records a presentation instead of writing an essay is doing the same argumentative work through a different modality. If the presentation path is structurally less demanding — shorter, lower evidentiary standard, no counterargument required — the board is tracking preference, not providing access.
beats.new_visual_element: UDL principle — same demand / different access / modality varies / intellectual labor does not

beats.narration_text: Claude cannot run the demand check — it can draft the paths, but demand equivalence requires domain knowledge about what the learning outcome requires. The audit question for every path: does this option require the student to construct an argument, marshal evidence, and address a counterargument? More questions, longer output, and richer media do not indicate higher demand. That judgment is yours.
beats.new_visual_element: Demand check — Claude drafts / teacher audits / construct argument / marshal evidence / address counterargument

beats.narration_text: The equity check is behavioral: if students consistently choose one path, ask why. Preference is fine. Consistent preference toward the structurally easier option is a signal. The choice board is not equitable if one path is easier to complete without the target learning. The fix is not removing that option — it is redesigning it until it requires the same intellectual work at a different access point.
beats.new_visual_element: Equity check — consistent choice = signal / redesign until demand is equal / different access, same work

beats.narration_text: Claude is fast at generating choice board options. The speed is the risk — three options in thirty seconds, published without audit. The demand check takes fifteen minutes. It requires your knowledge of the learning outcome, the evidence standard, and what the assignment is for. Claude drafts the board. You run the audit. Both are required.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — Claude drafts / teacher audits demand / equity check / both required before publish

beats.narration_text: Draft the board, then run this audit before you publish.

props.command: I have a UDL choice board: [paste options]. My learning outcome is: [state it]. Audit each option: (1) Does it require constructing an argument? (2) Does it require evidence from source material? (3) Does it require addressing a counterargument? (4) Could a student complete it without demonstrating the learning outcome? Flag any option that does not require all three, and suggest a redesign that preserves the modality while raising the demand to match.

beats.narration_text: If this catches a lower-demand path before students find it, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
